▼ Architecture & Changes

Architecture

▼
Click a zone to filter findings • Click background to reset
3
Blue circle = files changed in zone
Factory infrastructure
Product code
Infrastructure & docs
Click zone to filter • click background to reset

What Changed

▼

Generated from git diff by delegated diff-reading agent. Code diffs are ground truth.

Infrastructure: The CI refresh workflow (.github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml) is simplified from a 22-line inline bash block with a duplicated provider map to a single make refresh-profiles delegation. The canonical provider-to-directory mapping now lives in libs/model-profiles/Makefile as the PROFILE_PROVIDERS variable, making the refresh operation runnable locally without CI. The workflow retains its daily cron schedule, GitHub App token generation, and automated PR creation unchanged.
Product: The model profile schema is expanded with 7 new metadata fields from the models.dev API: name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, and temperature. These fields were previously fetched but silently dropped by _model_data_to_profile(). All 10 partner _profiles.py data files are regenerated with the enriched metadata (+2,257 lines of profile data across anthropic, deepseek, fireworks, groq, huggingface, mistralai, openai, openrouter, perplexity, xai). Two new unit tests are added to cover the field mapping and None-filtering behavior, though both have gaps: the temperature field lacks an assertion, and the omit-fields test asserts absence of fields that could never appear rather than the newly-added ones.

CI/CD

The refresh_model_profiles.yml workflow loses 22 lines of inline bash (the provider iteration loop with its associative array) and gains a single make refresh-profiles call. This is a clean DRY refactor: CI orchestrates, the Makefile owns the how. The workflow's other steps (checkout, uv setup, App token generation, PR creation via peter-evans/create-pull-request) are untouched. Pre-existing supply chain concerns remain: actions/checkout@v6 references a non-existent tag, and actions/create-github-app-token@v2 is pinned by mutable tag rather than SHA despite receiving the bot private key secret.

Model Profiles (Makefile)

The Makefile gains a refresh-profiles target (+33 lines) containing the provider map and iteration loop. The target overrides UV_FROZEN=false for the network-requiring API call while the rest of the Makefile enforces frozen deps. A latent portability issue exists: the ${partner//-/_} bash substitution syntax will silently fail under POSIX sh (Make's default shell on Ubuntu), though no current partner directory name triggers it.

Model Profiles (CLI + Tests)

The _model_data_to_profile function in cli.py gains 7 new model_data.get() calls (+7 lines). The implementation is straightforward passthrough with no special casing. Two new tests in test_cli.py (+64 lines): test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields exercises the new fields but omits an assertion for temperature; test_model_data_to_profile_omits_absent_fields checks for absence of fields like family and knowledge_cutoff that are never mapped in the implementation, making those assertions vacuous. The temperature field also introduces a naming ambiguity: it is a boolean capability flag (does the model accept temperature?) but shares a name with the universally-understood float sampling parameter.

Partner Integrations (Data Files)

All 10 partner _profiles.py files are regenerated with the new metadata fields. These are auto-generated Python dict literals with no executable logic. The largest change is openrouter (+1,202 lines, reflecting its large model catalog). The data is the expected downstream output of the model-profiles CLI and requires no independent tests.

Key Decisions

▼
1
Consolidate provider iteration logic from CI into Makefile
Eliminates duplication between the GitHub Actions workflow and local developer usage by making the Makefile the single source of truth for provider mappings.

The provider map (partner directory name to models.dev provider ID) previously existed only inline in the CI workflow YAML. Any developer running a local refresh had to replicate this mapping manually. By moving it to a PROFILE_PROVIDERS variable in the Makefile, the PR establishes one canonical location that both CI and local development share. The workflow step becomes a one-line make refresh-profiles delegation. This follows the principle that CI should orchestrate, not own business logic.

model-profiles ci-cd
FileChange
libs/model-profiles/MakefileAdded refresh-profiles target with PROFILE_PROVIDERS map (+33 lines)
.github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.ymlReplaced 22-line inline bash block with single make refresh-profiles call (-22/+1 lines)
2
Pass through models.dev field names directly without renaming
Minimizes transformation complexity and maintains a 1:1 mapping with the upstream API, at the cost of a naming ambiguity for the temperature field.

All 7 new fields use direct model_data.get(key) calls with no renaming or transformation. This preserves a clean mapping between models.dev API fields and profile keys. The tradeoff is that temperature as a boolean capability flag ("does this model accept temperature?") collides with the universally-understood float sampling parameter. Consumers reading profile["temperature"] may assume a float value. The alternative would have been to rename it to supports_temperature or temperature_supported, but the PR chose upstream fidelity over downstream clarity.

model-profiles
FileChange
libs/model-profiles/langchain_model_profiles/cli.pyAdded 7 new model_data.get() calls in _model_data_to_profile, preserving upstream field names
3
Bypass interactive safety prompt via echo y pipe in automation
The Makefile refresh target runs across 10 providers writing to relative paths outside the CWD, requiring automated confirmation of the safety prompt.

The CLI's _validate_data_dir function prompts the user when writing outside the current working directory. Since the refresh target writes to ../partners/*/, every invocation triggers this prompt. The Makefile pipes echo y to auto-confirm. This pattern was inherited from the old CI workflow but is now promoted to the user-visible Makefile, normalizing the bypass for local developer use. A cleaner approach would be a --yes or --non-interactive CLI flag that explicitly opts into non-interactive mode, preserving the prompt for ad-hoc usage.

model-profiles
FileChange
libs/model-profiles/Makefileecho y piped to CLI invocation to auto-confirm directory safety prompt
libs/model-profiles/langchain_model_profiles/cli.py_validate_data_dir interactive prompt is the safety gate being bypassed

No review findings in this zone.
FileCHSETIADAR+/−Zone
.github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml—B——A+1 −22ci-cd
CH—Code Health
No comments on this file.
SEBSecurity

At line 43 of .github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml:

uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v2

This step receives two secrets: MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_APP_ID and MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY. The GitHub App private key grants the ability to create installation tokens that can write to the repository (this token is then passed to peter-evans/create-pull-request).

Using a mutable tag (@v2) rather than an immutable commit SHA means the action's code could be changed without any change to the workflow file. If the actions/create-github-app-token repository is compromised and the v2 tag is moved to a malicious commit, the private key would be exfiltrated on the next scheduled run.

The same workflow correctly pins peter-evans/create-pull-request by SHA (@c0f553fe549906ede9cf27b5156039d195d2ece0), demonstrating awareness of this practice. The inconsistency is the concern.

Recommended fix: Pin actions/create-github-app-token to a specific commit SHA, e.g. uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v2.0.0 → look up the SHA for v2.0.0 and use actions/create-github-app-token@<SHA> # v2.

At line 25 of .github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml:

uses: actions/checkout@v6

As of the knowledge cutoff, actions/checkout has releases up to v4. A v6 tag does not exist. When GitHub Actions encounters a non-existent tag reference, resolution behavior is undefined — it may fail, fall back to a default, or (if a tag is later created by a malicious actor) resolve to unintended code.

This is a pre-existing issue not introduced by this PR. The PR does not modify the actions/checkout reference. However, since this PR modifies the same file, the opportunity to fix it is present.

Recommended fix: Change to actions/checkout@v4 (or the current latest) pinned by SHA. E.g. actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683 # v4.

TI—Test Integrity
No comments on this file.
AD—Adversarial
No comments on this file.
ARAArchitecture

Previously, the provider map (partner name → models.dev provider ID) existed only inline in .github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml. Any developer wanting to run a refresh locally had to replicate that logic manually.

This PR moves the canonical provider mapping and iteration loop into libs/model-profiles/Makefile as the refresh-profiles target. The workflow now delegates to make refresh-profiles — one line. This is the correct architectural direction: CI orchestrates, the Makefile owns the how.

The UV_FROZEN=false override in the Makefile target is appropriate — the refresh command requires network access by design and cannot use a frozen lockfile for the API call, while the rest of the Makefile targets enforce UV_FROZEN=true for reproducibility.

libs/model-profiles/MakefileBC———+33 −1model-profiles
CHBCode Health

The refresh-profiles target in libs/model-profiles/Makefile contains this line:

data_dir="../partners/$${partner}/langchain_$${partner//-/_}/data"

The syntax ${partner//-/_} is a bash global substitution (replace all - with _). POSIX sh (dash, the Ubuntu default /bin/sh) does not support this construct. Under dash, the expansion silently evaluates to an empty string or is treated literally, producing an incorrect path.

This was cargo-culted from the old GitHub Actions workflow step, which ran under bash (GitHub Actions uses bash by default). Moving the logic to a Makefile target changed the shell context without updating the syntax.

Currently, no partner directory name in the PROFILE_PROVIDERS list contains a hyphen (fireworks-ai is a provider ID passed to --provider, not a directory name — the directory is fireworks), so the substitution is a no-op and the bug is dormant. However, if a partner whose directory name contains a hyphen is added (e.g., azure-ai), the path construction silently breaks.

Fix: either add .SHELLFLAGS := -c and SHELL := /bin/bash at the top of the Makefile, or replace the bash substitution with POSIX-safe tr: data_dir="../partners/$${partner}/langchain_$(echo $${partner} | tr '-' '_')/data". The simpler fix is to rely on the fact that no partner directory currently has a hyphen and document the constraint explicitly, or switch the Makefile to bash.

The core refactor moves a 22-line bash block from .github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml into libs/model-profiles/Makefile as the refresh-profiles target. This is the right structural direction: the provider list is now in one place, runnable locally without CI, and the workflow step becomes a single-line delegation to make refresh-profiles.

The existing code quality practices are preserved:

  • The atomic write in _write_profiles_file (tempfile + replace) guards against partial writes on failure.
  • The UV_FROZEN=false override on the uv run invocation correctly handles the fact that profile refresh is a network-required operation while the rest of the Makefile operates with frozen deps.
  • The None-filtering idiom {k: v for k, v in profile.items() if v is not None} correctly preserves False and 0 values (which are meaningful capability flags) while dropping absent fields.
  • All new fields (name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, temperature) are mapped as simple passthroughs via model_data.get(), consistent with the existing pattern for all other fields in the function.

The provider list in PROFILE_PROVIDERS is consistent with the set of profile files updated in this PR (10 providers, exact match).

SECSecurity

The _validate_data_dir function in cli.py (line 19) checks whether the resolved data directory falls outside the current working directory and, if so, prints a warning and prompts input("Continue? (y/N): "). This is the only interactive safety gate against accidental writes to arbitrary paths.

The newly added refresh-profiles Makefile target (lines 29–37) invokes the CLI as:

echo y | UV_FROZEN=false uv run langchain-profiles refresh ...

This unconditionally answers "y" to the prompt, bypassing it for every provider, every run. The effect is that the safety prompt is dead code for all automated and make-driven invocations. A developer running make refresh-profiles from an unexpected working directory (e.g., the repo root rather than libs/model-profiles) would silently write _profiles.py files to wrong directories.

In CI this pattern was already present in the old workflow inline script (now removed). The concern is that it has been promoted into the user-visible Makefile, normalising the bypass for local developer use. The _ensure_safe_output_path symlink check still runs, but the directory-escape warning is fully circumvented.

Recommended fix: Add a --non-interactive or --yes flag to the CLI that explicitly opts into non-interactive mode; use that flag in the Makefile target instead of piping stdin. This preserves the prompt for ad-hoc local use while making the intent explicit in automation.

TI—Test Integrity
No comments on this file.
AD—Adversarial
No comments on this file.
AR—Architecture
No comments on this file.
libs/model-profiles/langchain_model_profiles/cli.pyB+BAAA+7 −0model-profiles
CHB+Code Health

The PR adds "temperature": model_data.get("temperature") to the profile dict in _model_data_to_profile. The models.dev API uses this field as a boolean capability flag: True means the model accepts a temperature parameter, False means it does not (e.g., reasoning models like codex-mini-latest have "temperature": False).

This is a naming ambiguity issue. In every LangChain interface, every OpenAI/Anthropic SDK, and every LLM paper, temperature is a float (typically 0.0–2.0). A developer reading profile["temperature"] in consuming code will assume it is a float and write code like if profile.get("temperature", 0.7) > 1.0: ... which will silently mis-branch when the value is True (Python's True > 1.0 is False, but the intent was wrong).

The field name is a direct passthrough from the upstream models.dev API, which constrains remediation options. Mitigation options: (1) rename the profile key to temperature_supported or supports_temperature at the CLI layer (with the augmentation system allowing override), or (2) add a comment in _model_data_to_profile and a docstring note clarifying the boolean semantics, and update the existing test to explicitly assert the type is bool.

SEBSecurity

The refresh function at line 242 fetches https://models.dev/api.json via httpx.get(api_url, timeout=30). httpx performs standard TLS certificate verification by default, which prevents passive MITM. However, there is no response integrity check beyond basic JSON parsing and a structural assertion (isinstance(all_data, dict)).

The fetched data is transformed by _model_data_to_profile and then serialised as Python source via json.dumps + string replacement into _profiles.py. These files are committed to the repository and later published as part of partner packages (e.g., langchain-openai, langchain-anthropic).

The threat model: if models.dev is compromised, the daily automated refresh workflow (refresh_model_profiles.yml) would open a pull request containing attacker-controlled data in these Python files. Since the files are auto-generated and the PR is created by a bot, reviewer attention may be lower. The data itself is a Python dict literal, so arbitrary code injection would require breaking the json.dumps serialisation — which the current code does not prevent if the API returned a crafted string value (e.g., a model name containing """ malicious_code() """ that escaped the JSON serialisation).

In practice, the json.dumps + .replace("true", "True") pipeline preserves JSON string escaping for most values, limiting immediate code injection risk. The severity is therefore B (weakness, limited exploitability) rather than F, but the supply chain dependency on an external third-party API with no integrity anchor is a structural weakness worth documenting.

Recommended mitigations: (1) Add a SHA-256 or similar hash of the known-good API response into the repository as a baseline, and fail loudly if the response deviates structurally. (2) Consider a CODEOWNERS or mandatory human review requirement for changes to _profiles.py files generated by the bot PR.

A thorough review of all 15 changed files found no instances of the following high-severity patterns:

  • Hardcoded secrets: No API keys, tokens, passwords, or credentials in source code or config. The workflow correctly uses ${{ secrets.MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_APP_ID }} and ${{ secrets.MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY }} via GitHub Secrets.
  • Command injection: No subprocess calls with shell=True. The Makefile shell loop uses only hardcoded provider names from a controlled static list (PROFILE_PROVIDERS), not from user or API input.
  • Deserialization: No pickle.load, torch.load, or custom __reduce__/__getstate__ methods. TOML parsing via tomllib is safe (declarative format, no code execution).
  • Eval/exec on external input: The importlib.util.spec_from_file_location + exec_module pattern in tests (lines 202–208, 263–269 of test_cli.py) operates on files written by the system under test using mocked API data — the input is controlled within the test and uses pytest's tmp_path fixture, which is the safe mechanism for this pattern.
  • Path traversal: _ensure_safe_output_path enforces that the output file resolves within base_dir, rejects symlinks at both the directory and file level, and calls sys.exit(1) on violation.
  • Generated _profiles.py files: All 10 modified partner profile files are pure Python dict literals with string/boolean/integer values and a single from typing import Any import. No executable logic is present.
TIATest Integrity

libs/model-profiles/langchain_model_profiles/cli.py (+7 lines): The 7 new fields in _model_data_to_profile are exercised by test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields (new) and test_model_data_to_profile_omits_absent_fields (new). Six of the seven new fields have explicit assertions; temperature is the exception (see finding test-integrity-1). The broader refresh() function that calls this transformation is tested by the pre-existing integration tests.

libs/model-profiles/Makefile (+33 lines): New refresh-profiles target. Shell script logic — not testable as a unit test. The CI workflow tests this target implicitly on every scheduled run. No action required.

.github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml (-22 lines): CI simplification — delegates to make refresh-profiles instead of inlining the loop. Correct refactoring. No unit test applies.

libs/model-profiles/uv.lock: Dependency lock file — auto-generated, no test applies.

libs/partners/*/data/_profiles.py (10 files, ~1100 net lines): Auto-generated data files. Correctness is validated by the CLI unit tests that exercise the generator. Individual data file tests would be brittle and are not expected by LangChain project conventions.

ADAAdversarial

The core implementation change — adding name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, and temperature fields to _model_data_to_profile — uses straightforward model_data.get(key) calls for all seven new fields. There is no hardcoding, no lookup table, and no test-environment detection.

The generated _profiles.py files across all ten providers correctly reflect the upstream data: name and release_date appear in every entry across all providers; open_weights is correctly stored as False (not filtered out, since False != None); temperature and attachment appear for providers where models.dev supplies them. The status field being absent from all generated profiles is consistent with models.dev not supplying a status key for these providers — it is filtered by the existing if v is not None logic.

The Makefile refresh-profiles target uses ../partners/ relative to libs/model-profiles/, which resolves to the same path as the old workflow's ../../libs/partners/. The consolidation is correct and reduces duplication between CI and local developer workflows.

ARAArchitecture

The profile schema expansion touches three layers within the model-profiles zone:

  1. cli.py — the transformation logic (_model_data_to_profile) gains new field mappings
  2. tests/unit_tests/test_cli.py — two new tests cover the new fields
  3. Makefile — the refresh target enables regeneration

The resulting data changes flow into partners zone data files (_profiles.py for 10 providers) but this is the expected write path: model-profiles CLI generates data consumed by the partners zone. This is a clean producer–consumer relationship with no circular dependency.

New fields added: name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, temperature. All are metadata fields from models.dev that were previously being silently dropped. The change makes the profile data richer and more complete without altering the profile file format or any consumer interfaces.

libs/model-profiles/tests/unit_tests/test_cli.pyB—BB—+64 −0model-profiles
CHBCode Health

The new test test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields is named to assert complete field coverage, and the input dict includes "temperature": True alongside "attachment": True. The test correctly asserts profile["attachment"] is True, but there is no corresponding assertion for profile["temperature"].

This is a misleading test: its name promises comprehensive coverage, and any reader trusting the name would believe temperature is verified. In reality, a regression that removes the "temperature": model_data.get("temperature") line from _model_data_to_profile would not be caught by this test.

Fix: add assert profile["temperature"] is True to the assertions block, parallel to the attachment assertion that is already present.

SE—Security
No comments on this file.
TIBTest Integrity

The diff adds "temperature": model_data.get("temperature") to _model_data_to_profile in cli.py (line ~128). The new test test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields correctly tests the six other new fields (name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment) but omits any assertion for temperature.

The test input already includes "temperature": True — adding assert profile["temperature"] is True in the Capabilities block (after line 314) is the minimal fix. Without it, deleting the temperature key from the implementation leaves all tests green.

The implementation ends with return {k: v for k, v in profile.items() if v is not None}. This correctly retains False because False is not None. However, the new test test_model_data_to_profile_omits_absent_fields only verifies that absent fields are dropped — it does not verify that a present False value is kept.

A concrete at-risk scenario: if a future refactor changes the filter to if v (testing truthiness instead of identity), open_weights=False, temperature=False, and reasoning_output=False would all silently vanish from every generated profile. No existing test would catch this regression.

Fix: add a test case with "open_weights": False and/or "tool_call": False in the model data, then assert the key is present in the returned profile with value False. The existing test_model_data_to_profile_text_modalities incidentally exercises text_inputs=False (computed from modalities), which is better than nothing, but the gap for explicitly-fetched boolean fields like open_weights and temperature remains.

The PR adds metadata fields to the generated profiles for all 10 partner packages (anthropic, deepseek, fireworks, groq, huggingface, mistralai, openai, openrouter, perplexity, xai). The generated files themselves are auto-generated artifacts and do not need individual tests.

However, the end-to-end path — CLI receives API response, merges with augmentations, writes file, file is importable as a Python module with the new keys — is only partially covered. test_refresh_generates_profiles_file imports the generated module but only asserts model IDs and augmentation key names are present as substrings in the raw text. It does not assert that the name, release_date, or open_weights fields appear in the structured _PROFILES dict.

The unit test test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields covers the transformation logic directly, which is the more important test. The end-to-end gap is minor but means a serialization bug (e.g., new keys silently dropped during JSON → Python conversion) would not be caught. Suggested improvement: add one assertion in test_refresh_generates_profiles_file such as assert "release_date" in profiles_content to anchor the round-trip.

ADBAdversarial

The new test test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields includes "temperature": True in its input model_data fixture, but the assertion block never checks profile["temperature"] is True. The test covers every other new field added in this PR (name, status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment) but silently skips temperature.

This means the mapping line "temperature": model_data.get("temperature") in _model_data_to_profile is not directly validated by any assertion. A future refactor that removed or mis-named this mapping would not be caught by this test. The generated _profiles.py data files show temperature appearing correctly in all providers (e.g. 195 occurrences in the openrouter file), so the production output is correct — but the test gap is a regression risk for the temperature field specifically.

The new test test_model_data_to_profile_omits_absent_fields is meant to verify that the None-filtering logic ({k: v for k, v in profile.items() if v is not None}) works for the new fields. However, its assertions check for the absence of family, knowledge_cutoff, cost_input, and interleaved — fields that do not exist anywhere in _model_data_to_profile and could never appear in the output under any code path.

The assertions will pass trivially regardless of what the implementation does. What the test should check is that the new fields (status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, temperature, and name) are absent when the minimal fixture omits them from the source data. None of these assertions are present. As written, this test does not exercise the None-filtering behavior for any of the fields introduced in this PR.

This is not gaming the system intentionally, but it does represent a pattern where the test was written to describe correct behavior in prose without actually validating that behavior through assertions that could catch a regression.

.claude/* (3 files)————B+0 −0
CH—Code Health
No comments on this file.
SE—Security
No comments on this file.
TI—Test Integrity
No comments on this file.
AD—Adversarial
No comments on this file.
ARBArchitecture

The zone registry defines 8 zones (core, langchain, model-profiles, partners, standard-tests, text-splitters, ci-cd, docs) and none have specs entries. Every zone carries specs: [].

While this PR does not introduce the gap, it also does not address it despite modifying 3 zones. The zone registry is the collaboration interface between the user and the review skill — zones without specs make it impossible to evaluate spec compliance, tie architecture decisions to documented intent, or validate that changes meet design requirements.

Recommendation: at minimum, add a specs reference to model-profiles (e.g., a link to the CLAUDE.md model-profiles section or a dedicated design doc) and to partners (the CLAUDE.md partner integration section). The ci-cd zone can point to CLAUDE.md's CI/CD infrastructure section.

.claude/zone-registry.yaml————B++0 −0
CH—Code Health
No comments on this file.
SE—Security
No comments on this file.
TI—Test Integrity
No comments on this file.
AD—Adversarial
No comments on this file.
ARB+Architecture

From CLAUDE.md: "libs/langchain/ — langchain-classic (legacy, no new features)" and "libs/langchain_v1/ — Actively maintained langchain package". These have distinct maintenance lifecycles and development policies, yet the zone registry groups them together under a single langchain zone with paths: ["libs/langchain/**", "libs/langchain_v1/**"].

This does not affect this PR (neither path is in the diff), but it is an architectural documentation gap: the zone registry conflates two distinct components that have different intended change policies. A reviewer reading zone coverage cannot tell from the zone whether a change touches the legacy package or the active one.

Recommendation: consider splitting into langchain-legacy and langchain-v1 zones, or at minimum update the sublabel to note the distinction. This PR is not the right vehicle for this change, but it should be tracked.

Thresholds: ✓ under 1m = normal • ○ 1-5m = acceptable • ⚠ 5-10m = watch • ✖ over 10m = needs refactoring

▼ Follow-ups

Post-Merge Items

▼
MEDIUM Add missing <code>temperature</code> assertion in capture test

Three independent reviewers (code-health, test-integrity, adversarial) flagged the same gap: test_model_data_to_profile_captures_all_models_dev_fields supplies "temperature": True in its input but never asserts the output contains it. This is the only new field without test coverage. The test name promises comprehensive field coverage, making the gap misleading to future contributors.

## libs/model-profiles/tests/unit_tests/test_cli.py, lines 310-315 # Capabilities assert profile["tool_calling"] is True assert profile["structured_output"] is True assert profile["attachment"] is True # Missing: assert profile["temperature"] is True
Failure scenario
A refactor removes the temperature mapping from _model_data_to_profile. All tests pass. The temperature field silently disappears from all 10 partner _profiles.py files on the next refresh. Downstream consumers that depend on the temperature capability flag get None instead of True/False.
Resolution
Add assert profile["temperature"] is True to the Capabilities block of the test. A one-line fix that locks down the mapping.
model-profiles
MEDIUM Replace vacuous assertions in omit-fields test with meaningful ones

The test_model_data_to_profile_omits_absent_fields test asserts that family, knowledge_cutoff, cost_input, and interleaved are absent from the profile. None of these fields are mapped anywhere in the codebase, so these assertions pass trivially regardless of implementation. The test should instead verify that the newly-added fields (status, release_date, last_updated, open_weights, attachment, temperature) are correctly absent when not supplied.

## libs/model-profiles/tests/unit_tests/test_cli.py, lines 340-353 assert "family" not in profile assert "knowledge_cutoff" not in profile assert "cost_input" not in profile assert "interleaved" not in profile
Failure scenario
A refactor breaks the None-filtering logic (e.g., changes if v is not None to if v). Fields with False values (open_weights, temperature) silently disappear from generated profiles. No test catches it because the omit-fields test only checks unmapped fields.
Resolution
Replace the four vacuous assertions with assertions for the actual new fields: assert "status" not in profile, assert "release_date" not in profile, etc. Add a companion test with open_weights=False to verify False values survive the None filter.
model-profiles
LOW Pin <code>actions/create-github-app-token</code> by SHA in CI workflow

The refresh workflow uses actions/create-github-app-token@v2 (mutable tag) while the adjacent peter-evans/create-pull-request action is correctly SHA-pinned. The unpinned action receives the MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY secret. This inconsistency was pre-existing but the workflow file is open in this PR.

## .github/workflows/refresh_model_profiles.yml, lines 43-47 - name: "Generate GitHub App token" id: app-token uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v2 with: app-id: ${{ secrets.MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_APP_ID }} private-key: ${{ secrets.MODEL_PROFILE_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY }}
Failure scenario
A supply chain attack moves the v2 tag to a malicious commit. The daily automated workflow exfiltrates the GitHub App private key, granting write access to the repository.
Resolution
Pin to a specific commit SHA: uses: actions/create-github-app-token@<SHA> # v2. Matches the pinning practice already applied to peter-evans/create-pull-request in the same file.
ci-cd
LOW Consider adding a <code>--yes</code> CLI flag to replace <code>echo y</code> pipe pattern

The Makefile pipes echo y to auto-confirm the CLI's interactive safety prompt when writing outside the CWD. This pattern, inherited from the old CI workflow, defeats the safety gate for all make refresh-profiles invocations including local developer use. An explicit --yes or --non-interactive flag would make the intent clear and preserve the prompt for ad-hoc CLI usage.

Failure scenario
A developer runs make refresh-profiles from an unexpected working directory. The safety prompt is silently auto-confirmed, and _profiles.py files are written to wrong paths without any warning being visible.
Resolution
The CLI accepts a --yes flag that skips the interactive prompt. The Makefile uses --yes explicitly. Ad-hoc langchain-profiles refresh invocations still show the safety prompt.
model-profiles
